blocks:
Surah Al-Ikhlas

Meccan, and it is said Medinan. It is named Surah Al-Tawhid (The Surah of Monotheism), and it consists of five verses. بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful)

(١) قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ. (1) Say: He is Allah, the One. (٢) اللهُ الصَّمَدُ. (2) Allah is the All-sufficient. (٣) لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ. (3) He neither begets, nor is begotten, (٤)** وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ ** (4) nor has He any equal.

And it was read كُفْؤًا (kuf'an) with taskin (vowel suppression) and with tahrik (vowel movement) and by changing the hamzah into a waw. Al-Qummi said: The reason for its revelation was that the Jews came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and asked him, "What is the lineage (نسبة) of your Lord?" So Allah revealed [it].

In al-Kafi and al-Tawhid, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said: The Jews asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, saying, "Describe the lineage (انسب) of your Lord to us." He remained silent for three days, not answering them, then قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ (Say: He is Allah) was revealed until the end of the Surah.

In al-Tawhid, narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding its interpretation, he said: قُلْ (Say) means: "Manifest what We have revealed to you and what We have informed you of, by composing the letters which We have recited to you, so that whoever lends an ear while being a witness (شَهِيدٌ) may be guided by them." And هُوَ (He) is a pronominal noun (اسم مكنّى) pointing to an Absent One (غائب). The hā’ (الهاء) is an alert to an established meaning (معنى ثابت), and the waw (الواو) is an indication of the One Absent from the senses (الحواس), just as your saying هذا (this) is an indication of the Present One (الشاهد) to the senses. This is because the disbelievers drew attention to their deities using a letter that points to the perceived Present One, saying: "These are our deities, perceptible and apprehended by sight. So, O Muhammad, point to your God Whom you call to, so that we may see Him and apprehend Him, and not be bewildered (نأله) concerning Him." So Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed: قُلْ هُوَ (Say: He is). The hā’ (الهاء) is an affirmation of the Established One (الثابت), and the waw (الواو) is an indication of the One Absent from the perception of sights and the touch of the senses. And He is Exalted above that; rather, He is the Perceiver of sights and the Originator of the senses.

Then he عليه السلام said: اللهُ (Allah) means the Worshipped One (المعبود) concerning Whom creation is bewildered (الِهَ) from perceiving what comes from Him and from encompassing His nature (كيفيّته). The Arabs say أَلِهَ الرَّجُلُ (the man was bewildered) when he is confused about something and cannot encompass it with knowledge, and وَلِهَ (he was distressed) when he seeks refuge in something he fears and dreads. And الْإِلَهُ (The God) is the One concealed from the senses of creation.

He عليه السلام said: الْأَحَدُ (The One) is the Singular (الفرد), the Unique (المتفرّد). الْأَحَدُ and الْوَاحِدُ (The One) have the same meaning, and He is the Unique One Who has no peer (نظير). الـتَّوْحِيدُ (Monotheism) is the affirmation of unity (الْوَحْدَة), which is singularity (الْاِنْفِرَاد). And الْوَاحِدُ is the Distinct One (المباين) Who does not emanate from anything and does not unite with anything. Hence, they said that the structure of number begins with the one (الْوَاحِد), but the one is not part of the number (الْعَدَد), because number does not apply to the one, but rather applies to the two. Thus, the meaning of His Almighty saying اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Allah, the One) is: The Worshipped One (المعبود) concerning Whom creation is bewildered (يَأْلَهُ) from perceiving Him and encompassing His nature (كيفيّته), Singular in His Divinity (إلهيّته), Exalted above the attributes of His creation.

He عليه السلام said: And my father, Zayn al-Abidin, narrated to me from his father, Al-Husayn ibn Ali عليهم السلام, that he said: الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient) is the One Who has no cavity (جوف), and الصَّمَدُ is the One Whose sovereignty (سودده) has reached its limit, and الصَّمَدُ is the One Who neither eats nor drinks, and الصَّمَدُ is the One Who does not sleep, and الصَّمَدُ is the Everlasting One (الدائم) Who has always been and will always be.

He عليه السلام said: Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah used to say: الصَّمَدُ is the Self-Subsisting One (القائم بنفسه), Independent of others (الغني عن غيره). And others said: الصَّمَدُ is the One Exalted above existence (الكون) and corruption (الفساد), and الصَّمَدُ is the One Who is not described by change (التّغاير).

He عليه السلام said: الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient) is the Obedient Master (السيّد المطاع) above Whom there is no commander or prohibitor.

He said: And Ali ibn al-Husayn عليهما السلام was asked about الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient), and he replied: الصَّمَدُ is the One Who has no partner, and the preservation of anything does not weary Him, nor is anything hidden from Him.

The narrator said: Zayd ibn Ali عليه السلام said: الصَّمَدُ is the One Who, when He wills a thing, says to it, "Be," and it is. And الصَّمَدُ is the One Who originated things, creating them as opposites (أضداد), varieties (أصناف), forms (أشكال), and pairs (أزواج), and He is unique in unity (الوحدة) without an opposite (ضدّ), or a form (شكل), or a likeness (مثل), or a rival (ندّ).

He said: And al-Sadiq narrated to me from his father عليهما السلام: The people of Basra wrote to Al-Husayn ibn Ali عليهما السلام asking him about الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient). So he wrote to them: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful). To proceed: Do not delve into the Quran, nor dispute concerning it, nor speak about it without knowledge. I heard my grandfather, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, say: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran without knowledge, let him take his seat in the Fire." And Allah, the Glorified, has indeed interpreted الصَّمَدُ by saying: اللهُ أَحَدٌ اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah, the One. Allah is the All-sufficient). Then He interpreted it further by saying: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (He neither begets, nor is begotten, nor has He any equal). لَمْ يَلِدْ (He neither begets): Nothing dense (كثيف) comes forth from Him, like a child or other dense things that come forth from created beings, nor anything subtle (لطيف) like the soul (النّفس). Nor do sudden changes (البدوات) (1) branch off from Him, such as drowsiness (السّنة), sleep, passing thought (الخطرة), worry (الهمّ), sorrow (الحزن), joy (البهجة), laughter, weeping, fear, hope, desire (الرّغبة), weariness (السّآمة), hunger, or satiety. He is Exalted above anything coming forth from Him, or anything dense or subtle being generated from Him. وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (nor is He begotten): He was not generated from anything, nor did He come forth from anything, just as dense things come forth from their elements, such as a thing from a thing, an animal from an animal, plants from the earth, water from springs, and fruits from trees. Nor like subtle things coming forth from their centers, such as sight from the eye, hearing from the ear, smell from the nose, taste from the mouth, speech from the tongue, and knowledge and discernment from the heart, or like fire from stone. Nay, rather He is اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah, the All-sufficient), Who is not from a thing, nor in a thing, nor upon a thing. He is the Originator (مبدع) of things, their Creator (خالقها), and the Initiator (منشئ) of things by His power. What He created for annihilation vanishes by His will, and what He created for permanence remains by His knowledge. That is اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah, the All-sufficient), Who لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ الْكَبِيرُ الْمُتَعالِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (neither begets, nor is begotten, the Knower of the unseen and the visible, the Great, the Exalted, nor has He any equal).

The narrator said: I heard al-Sadiq عليه السلام say: A delegation from Palestine came to Al-Baqir عليه السلام and asked him questions, and He answered them. Then they asked him about الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient). He replied: Its interpretation is contained within it. الصَّمَدُ has five letters. The alif (الالف) is evidence of His I-ness (أنيّته), which is His saying, the Mighty and Exalted: شَهِدَ اللهُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ (Allah bears witness that there is no god except Him). This is an alert and an indication to the One Absent from the perception of the senses. The lām (اللّام) is evidence of His Divinity (إلهيّته), that He is Allah. The alif and the lām are assimilated (مدغمان); they do not appear on the tongue and are not heard, but they appear in writing. They are two proofs that His Divinity, due to His subtlety (لطفه), is hidden, not perceived by the senses, and does not fall upon the tongue of a describer or the ear of a listener. This is because the interpretation of الْإِلَهُ (The God) is the One concerning Whom creation is bewildered (أَلِهَ) from perceiving His essence (ماهيته) and nature (كيفيته) by sense or by imagination (وهم). Nay, rather He is the Originator of imaginations (الأوهام) and the Creator of the senses. This only appears in writing, indicating that Allah, the Exalted, manifested His Lordship (ربوبيّته) in the origination of creation and the composition of their subtle souls (أرواحهم اللّطيفة) within their dense bodies (أجسادهم الكثيفة). Thus, when a servant looks at himself, he does not see his soul, just as the lām of الصَّمَدُ is not distinct and does not enter any of his five senses. But when he looks at the writing, what was hidden and subtle becomes apparent to him. Whenever the servant reflects upon the essence (ماهيّة) and nature (كيفيته) of the Creator (الْبَارِي), he is bewildered (أَلِهَ) and confused, and his thought cannot encompass anything that can be conceived for Him, because He, the Mighty and Exalted, is the Creator of forms (الصّور). But when he looks at His creation, it is established for him that He, the Mighty and Exalted, is their Creator and the Composer of their souls within their bodies. As for the ṣād (الصاد), it is evidence that He, the Mighty and Exalted, is Truthful (صادق), and His saying is truth (صدق), and His speech is truth (صدق). He called His servants to follow the truth (الصدق) with truth, and promised the Abode of Truth (دار الصدق) with truth. As for the mīm (الميم), it is evidence of His Sovereignty (ملكه), and that He is the True King (الملك الحقّ). He has always been and will always be, and His sovereignty will not cease. As for the dāl (الدّال), it is evidence of the permanence of His sovereignty (دوام ملكه), and that He, the Mighty and Exalted, is Everlasting (دائم), Exalted above existence (الكون) and cessation (الزوال). Rather, He, the Mighty and Exalted, is the Constitutor of existents (مكوّن الكائنات), by Whose constitution every existent came to be. Then he said: If I found those capable of bearing the knowledge that Allah, the Mighty and Exalted, has granted me, I would disseminate الـتَّوْحِيد (Monotheism), الْإِسْلَام (Submission), الْإِيمَان (Faith), الدِّين (Religion), and the Divine Laws (الشرايع) from الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient). But how can I do that when my grandfather, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, did not find those capable of bearing His knowledge, such that He would sigh deeply (يتنفّس الصّعداء) and say from the pulpit: "Ask me before you lose me, for between my ribs is abundant knowledge—hāh, hāh, hāh—but alas, I find no one to bear it! Indeed, I am the conclusive proof (الحجّة البالغة) from Allah upon you. So do not take as allies a people with whom Allah is wrathful: قَدْ يَئِسُوا مِنَ الْآخِرَةِ كَما يَئِسَ الْكُفَّارُ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْقُبُورِ (They have despaired of the Hereafter, just as the faithless have despaired of the occupants of the graves)."

Then Al-Baqir عليه السلام said: All praise belongs to Allah Who conferred favor upon us and granted us success in worshipping الْأَحَدُ الصَّمَدُ (The One, the All-sufficient), Who لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (neither begets, nor is begotten, nor has He any equal), and kept us away from the worship of idols—an everlasting praise and a continuous gratitude. And His saying, the Mighty and Exalted: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (He neither begets, nor is begotten) means: لَمْ يَلِدْ (He neither begets) so that He would have a child to inherit His sovereignty, وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (nor is He begotten) so that He would have a parent to share in His Lordship (ربوبيّته) and His sovereignty, and وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (nor has He any equal) so that someone might contend with Him in His dominion.

In al-Majma', narrated from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام: A man asked him about the interpretation of this Surah, and he replied: هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (He is Allah, the One) without the interpretation of number (عدد). الصَّمَدُ (The All-sufficient) without the division of parts (بدد). لَمْ يَلِدْ (He neither begets) so that He would be an inherited, perishable one. وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (nor is He begotten) so that He would be a shared god. وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ (nor has He) from His creation كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (any equal). And in Nahj al-Balaghah: لَمْ يُولَدْ (nor is He begotten) so that He would be a partner in glory (العزّ).

In al-Kafi, narrated from Al-Sajjad عليه السلام: He was asked about الـتَّوْحِيد (Monotheism), and he replied: Allah, the Mighty and Exalted, knew that there would be people in the Last Age who delve deeply (متعمّقون), so Allah revealed قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say: He is Allah, the One) and the verses from Surah Al-Hadid up to His saying عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ (Knower of what is in the breasts). Whoever seeks beyond that has perished.

And from Al-Rida عليه السلام: He was asked about الـتَّوْحِيد (Monotheism), and he replied: Everyone who recites قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say: He is Allah, the One) and believes in it has known الـتَّوْحِيد. It was asked: "How should he recite it?" He replied: "As people recite it, and he should add to it: كَذَلِكَ اللهُ رَبِّي (Thus is Allah, my Lord) twice."

And from Al-Baqir عليه السلام: قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say: He is Allah, the One) is one third of the Quran. And in al-Ikmal, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, he said: Whoever recites قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ once, it is as if he has recited one third of the Quran. And whoever recites it twice, it is as if he has recited two thirds of the Quran. And whoever recites it three times, it is as if he has recited the entire Quran.

In Thawab al-A'mal and al-Majma', narrated from Al-Sadiq عليه السلام: If a day passes for someone, and he performs five prayers in it, but does not recite قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say: He is Allah, the One) in them, it will be said to him: "O servant of Allah, you are not among those who pray."

And from him عليه السلام: Whoever has a Friday pass for him and does not recite قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say: He is Allah, the One) in it, and then dies, he dies upon the religion of Abu Lahab.

footnote (1) *ذو بدوات* (Possessor of *بدوات*): Meaning, He does not cease to have a new opinion (*رأي جديد*) appear to Him. From this comes the phrase *بدا له في الأمر* (it appeared to him concerning the matter) when the approval of something other than the first thing became apparent to him.
